Section heading: Our use of cookies

**Session 2022-23**

Section heading: Our use of cookies

**Session 2022-23**

chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written Evidence submitted by WebGroup Czech Republic, a.s. and NKL Associates s.r.o. (OSB82)**

**House of Commons Public Bill Committee**

**Online Safety Bill**

**Executive Summary**

· WGCZ fully supports initiatives to protect children from adult content, however the Online Safety Bill will not be effective at achieving this goal as it will fail to prevent children from accessing online adult content.

· All websites hosting adult content will inevitably be required to acquire, develop and utilise unspecified age verification technology in order to meet the obligations imposed upon them. The currently available age verification technologies are easily defeated or circumvented.

· The imposition of age verification will cause vast pools of personal data to be collated by private companies creating a mass, diffuse, private sector-led and unregulated surveillance system that can be used to track user movements online, determine fundamentally private and intimate human preferences, and create a link between online activity and identities.

· Collating such data invites cyber-attacks from those seeking to commit identity theft, blackmail, coercion or other related criminal activities. The data is also liable to be packaged and sold for the purposes of greater targeted advertising. The Online Safety Bill does not address these concerns at all.

· Members of the LGBT+ community are likely to be disproportionately impacted by suppressing expression and the risk of their sexuality being made public irrespective of their wishes.
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written Evidence submitted by WebGroup Czech Republic, a.s. and NKL Associates s.r.o. (OSB82)**

**House of Commons Public Bill Committee**

**Online Safety Bill**

**Executive Summary**

· WGCZ fully supports initiatives to protect children from adult content, however the Online Safety Bill will not be effective at achieving this goal as it will fail to prevent children from accessing online adult content.

· All websites hosting adult content will inevitably be required to acquire, develop and utilise unspecified age verification technology in order to meet the obligations imposed upon them. The currently available age verification technologies are easily defeated or circumvented.

· The imposition of age verification will cause vast pools of personal data to be collated by private companies creating a mass, diffuse, private sector-led and unregulated surveillance system that can be used to track user movements online, determine fundamentally private and intimate human preferences, and create a link between online activity and identities.

· Collating such data invites cyber-attacks from those seeking to commit identity theft, blackmail, coercion or other related criminal activities. The data is also liable to be packaged and sold for the purposes of greater targeted advertising. The Online Safety Bill does not address these concerns at all.

· Members of the LGBT+ community are likely to be disproportionately impacted by suppressing expression and the risk of their sexuality being made public irrespective of their wishes.

chunks.text: be packaged and sold for the purposes of greater targeted advertising. The Online Safety Bill does not address these concerns at all. · Members of the LGBT+ community are likely to be disproportionately impacted by suppressing expression and the risk of their sexuality being made public irrespective of their wishes. · The creation and/or expansion of an age verification technology sector will disproportionately impact micro, small and start-up companies who cannot leverage economies of scale in connection with the financial costs of such technology. This will lead to a stifling of innovation and, in the online adult content industry, may lead to the creation of a monopoly. · There are alternative solutions to ensuring child safety online that do not carry the same adverse effects on civil liberties, data protection, LGBT+ rights and the profiting from data capture. These include shifting the obligations to impose age restrictions to internet service providers and hardware manufactures, and creating an obligation that age restriction features are enabled as a default setting on devices, such as mobile devices, and ensuring that only an adult can disable those settings. · OFCOM research suggests that a significant proportion of internet users are unaware that functionality to restrict user access to certain content, such as ‘parental controls’ on domestic networks and personal devices, already exists and is widely available. Investment in educating parents, caregivers and children on the risks associated with internet use, and the services and functions already available to restrict access to certain content, would be a more effective harm reduction tool than anything proposed in the draft Online Safety Bill. **Introduction**
chunks.display_text: · The creation and/or expansion of an age verification technology sector will disproportionately impact micro, small and start-up companies who cannot leverage economies of scale in connection with the financial costs of such technology. This will lead to a stifling of innovation and, in the online adult content industry, may lead to the creation of a monopoly. · There are alternative solutions to ensuring child safety online that do not carry the same adverse effects on civil liberties, data protection, LGBT+ rights and the profiting from data capture. These include shifting the obligations to impose age restrictions to internet service providers and hardware manufactures, and creating an obligation that age restriction features are enabled as a default setting on devices, such as mobile devices, and ensuring that only an adult can disable those settings. · OFCOM research suggests that a significant proportion of internet users are unaware that functionality to restrict user access to certain content, such as ‘parental controls’ on domestic networks and personal devices, already exists and is widely available. Investment in educating parents, caregivers and children on the risks associated with internet use, and the services and functions already available to restrict access to certain content, would be a more effective harm reduction tool than anything proposed in the draft Online Safety Bill. **Introduction**

chunks.text: exists and is widely available. Investment in educating parents, caregivers and children on the risks associated with internet use, and the services and functions already available to restrict access to certain content, would be a more effective harm reduction tool than anything proposed in the draft Online Safety Bill. **Introduction** 1.We are WebGroup Czech Republic, a.s. (formerly WGCZ s.r.o.) and NKL Associates s.r.o., who specialize in the online hosting of adult content and own the websites xvideos.com and xnxx.com, respectively, (collectively " **WGCZ** " **or** " **we** "). We are providing written evidence to the Public Bill Committee as, due to the borderless nature of the internet, WGCZ will be, together with all other adult website hosting services around the world, directly impacted by the Draft Online Safety Bill (" **the Bill** ") if and when it is enacted. 2.WGCZ has written to \[the DCMS Sub-Committee on Online Harms and Disinformation and the Joint Select Committee on 3 September 2021 and 27 September 2021 respectively\] in connection with the Bill, and we invite the Public Bill Committee to review those submissions, which are enclosed here. \[1\] \[2\] 3.We previously set out in those submissions that WGCZ has significant concerns that the Bill will not effectively prevent harm or accomplish the aims it sets out to achieve. Moreover, the Bill represents a serious threat to free speech, would create a legal basis for censorship by social media companies, and represents an unwarranted extension of government power. The primary focus of this written evidence is section 68 " *Duties about regulated provider pornographic content* " as currently drafted.
chunks.display_text: 1.We are WebGroup Czech Republic, a.s. (formerly WGCZ s.r.o.) and NKL Associates s.r.o., who specialize in the online hosting of adult content and own the websites xvideos.com and xnxx.com, respectively, (collectively " **WGCZ** " **or** " **we** "). We are providing written evidence to the Public Bill Committee as, due to the borderless nature of the internet, WGCZ will be, together with all other adult website hosting services around the world, directly impacted by the Draft Online Safety Bill (" **the Bill** ") if and when it is enacted. 2.WGCZ has written to \[the DCMS Sub-Committee on Online Harms and Disinformation and the Joint Select Committee on 3 September 2021 and 27 September 2021 respectively\] in connection with the Bill, and we invite the Public Bill Committee to review those submissions, which are enclosed here. \[1\] \[2\] 3.We previously set out in those submissions that WGCZ has significant concerns that the Bill will not effectively prevent harm or accomplish the aims it sets out to achieve. Moreover, the Bill represents a serious threat to free speech, would create a legal basis for censorship by social media companies, and represents an unwarranted extension of government power. The primary focus of this written evidence is section 68 " *Duties about regulated provider pornographic content* " as currently drafted.

chunks.text: Moreover, the Bill represents a serious threat to free speech, would create a legal basis for censorship by social media companies, and represents an unwarranted extension of government power. The primary focus of this written evidence is section 68 " *Duties about regulated provider pornographic content* " as currently drafted. 4.We set out below the reasons why WGCZ considers this section will be ineffective at achieving the aim of protecting children from pornographic content, together with an overview of the potentially harmful effects to civil rights, data privacy, the LGBT+ community and the market place in general. **Section 68 Will Be Ineffective** 5.As currently drafted, section 68 would impose a duty on companies such as WGCZ to, " *ensure that children are not normally able to encounter content that is regulated provider pornographic content in relation to the service"* and suggests that this can be achieved, " *for example, by using age verification"*. Age verification is not a feature that is mandated by the Bill and instead the responsibility for issuing guidance on how websites can prevent children from accessing their content is left to OFCOM. However, considering the currently available technological options, age verification systems will inevitably be the only way a website would be able to meet this burden. The UK’s Information Commissioner’s Office guidance entitled *Age Appropriate Design: A Code of Practice For Online Services* already refers to various forms of age verification as the means by which websites can meet their duties to establish the age of their users in a data protection context.
chunks.display_text: 4.We set out below the reasons why WGCZ considers this section will be ineffective at achieving the aim of protecting children from pornographic content, together with an overview of the potentially harmful effects to civil rights, data privacy, the LGBT+ community and the market place in general. **Section 68 Will Be Ineffective** 5.As currently drafted, section 68 would impose a duty on companies such as WGCZ to, " *ensure that children are not normally able to encounter content that is regulated provider pornographic content in relation to the service"* and suggests that this can be achieved, " *for example, by using age verification"*. Age verification is not a feature that is mandated by the Bill and instead the responsibility for issuing guidance on how websites can prevent children from accessing their content is left to OFCOM. However, considering the currently available technological options, age verification systems will inevitably be the only way a website would be able to meet this burden. The UK’s Information Commissioner’s Office guidance entitled *Age Appropriate Design: A Code of Practice For Online Services* already refers to various forms of age verification as the means by which websites can meet their duties to establish the age of their users in a data protection context.

chunks.text: meet this burden. The UK’s Information Commissioner’s Office guidance entitled *Age Appropriate Design: A Code of Practice For Online Services* already refers to various forms of age verification as the means by which websites can meet their duties to establish the age of their users in a data protection context. 6.In any currently available age verification process, circumvention is very straightforward. Users can simply input inaccurate dates during a self-declaration process, provide falsified identity documents or documents that belong to an adult to a third party age verification service, or simply use the log in credentials created by an adult in order to access content that requires account holder verification. The Bill would not prevent a child from accessing inappropriate content by using the legitimate age verification credentials belonging to adults within their own household who allow access either knowingly or negligently. 7.Users can also use Virtual Private Networks (" **VPNs** "), which encrypt internet traffic and disguise a user’s geographical location, which would cause any jurisdiction based age verification requirement to remain untriggered. By using a VPN to cause a website to believe the user was in a country other than the UK, for example, the age verification process would not be required and therefore would be circumvented. VPN use is wide spread, is often provided as part of modern cybersecurity packages, and there are a large number of products available for download and use by anyone who uses the internet. Any other form of age verification process, such as artificial intelligence to determine the age of a user by reference to their interactions on a website, would plainly be entirely ineffective for any website whose content is exclusively for an adult audience.
chunks.display_text: 6.In any currently available age verification process, circumvention is very straightforward. Users can simply input inaccurate dates during a self-declaration process, provide falsified identity documents or documents that belong to an adult to a third party age verification service, or simply use the log in credentials created by an adult in order to access content that requires account holder verification. The Bill would not prevent a child from accessing inappropriate content by using the legitimate age verification credentials belonging to adults within their own household who allow access either knowingly or negligently. 7.Users can also use Virtual Private Networks (" **VPNs** "), which encrypt internet traffic and disguise a user’s geographical location, which would cause any jurisdiction based age verification requirement to remain untriggered. By using a VPN to cause a website to believe the user was in a country other than the UK, for example, the age verification process would not be required and therefore would be circumvented. VPN use is wide spread, is often provided as part of modern cybersecurity packages, and there are a large number of products available for download and use by anyone who uses the internet. Any other form of age verification process, such as artificial intelligence to determine the age of a user by reference to their interactions on a website, would plainly be entirely ineffective for any website whose content is exclusively for an adult audience.

chunks.text: and use by anyone who uses the internet. Any other form of age verification process, such as artificial intelligence to determine the age of a user by reference to their interactions on a website, would plainly be entirely ineffective for any website whose content is exclusively for an adult audience. 8.The imposition of age verification requirements on websites will only serve to generate revenue for third party providers of such systems, and will act as a fig leaf that can easily be subverted by any technologically literate user. The UK government’s policy in this context does not take account of the prevalence of adult material accessible via the internet and social media platforms, makes no proposals for increased education concerning safe internet use to parents, caregivers and children, and has not considered alternatives such as responsibilities on internet service providers and hardware manufactures. 9.For these reasons, the purpose of this section of the Bill will be wholly ineffective and that fundamentally undermines the goal of keeping children safe when online. **Potentially Harmful Effects**
chunks.display_text: 8.The imposition of age verification requirements on websites will only serve to generate revenue for third party providers of such systems, and will act as a fig leaf that can easily be subverted by any technologically literate user. The UK government’s policy in this context does not take account of the prevalence of adult material accessible via the internet and social media platforms, makes no proposals for increased education concerning safe internet use to parents, caregivers and children, and has not considered alternatives such as responsibilities on internet service providers and hardware manufactures. 9.For these reasons, the purpose of this section of the Bill will be wholly ineffective and that fundamentally undermines the goal of keeping children safe when online. **Potentially Harmful Effects**

chunks.text: to parents, caregivers and children, and has not considered alternatives such as responsibilities on internet service providers and hardware manufactures. 9.For these reasons, the purpose of this section of the Bill will be wholly ineffective and that fundamentally undermines the goal of keeping children safe when online. **Potentially Harmful Effects** 10.The inevitable reliance on age verification systems in order to meet the obligations created in the Bill will necessarily result in the increased use of third party supplied software, systems, management and verification services. Companies providing these services, who have thus far been unable to provide an effective product or any metrics by which success can be measured, will derive considerable profit. Those companies will also harvest enormous amounts of personal data that will result in a mass, diffuse, private sector-led and unregulated surveillance system that can be used to track user movements online, determine fundamentally private and intimate human preferences, and create a link between online activity and identities. This represents a dramatic increase in online surveillance that is entirely contra to British civil liberty principals, and that is an issue that many leading organizations in the fields of privacy and censorship rightly oppose.
chunks.display_text: 10.The inevitable reliance on age verification systems in order to meet the obligations created in the Bill will necessarily result in the increased use of third party supplied software, systems, management and verification services. Companies providing these services, who have thus far been unable to provide an effective product or any metrics by which success can be measured, will derive considerable profit. Those companies will also harvest enormous amounts of personal data that will result in a mass, diffuse, private sector-led and unregulated surveillance system that can be used to track user movements online, determine fundamentally private and intimate human preferences, and create a link between online activity and identities. This represents a dramatic increase in online surveillance that is entirely contra to British civil liberty principals, and that is an issue that many leading organizations in the fields of privacy and censorship rightly oppose.

chunks.text: fundamentally private and intimate human preferences, and create a link between online activity and identities. This represents a dramatic increase in online surveillance that is entirely contra to British civil liberty principals, and that is an issue that many leading organizations in the fields of privacy and censorship rightly oppose. 11.However robust a company’s cybersecurity systems are, vulnerabilities to attack will remain and motivated ‘hackers’ will always find a way to access digital information. The categories and nature of the personal data held by companies that collate age verification data will be extremely valuable to any unscrupulous individual, or those participating in organised crime, that would seek to use that information for criminal purposes, such as identity theft, fraud or related scams. This data could also be sold by the companies capturing it to others who would seek to utilize it for targeted advertising. There is currently no acceptable or sufficient privacy code governing the use of this data by age verification providers, and no provision for this in the Bill. 12.User data, particularly where it concerns preferences for adult content, could easily be used to extort or otherwise exercise leverage over an individual. It is entirely plausible that malicious state actors could utilise such information to exert influence or control over those occupying position of political or commercial power. The value of this data is clear and would make any company possessing it particularly likely to come under cyber-attack.
chunks.display_text: 11.However robust a company’s cybersecurity systems are, vulnerabilities to attack will remain and motivated ‘hackers’ will always find a way to access digital information. The categories and nature of the personal data held by companies that collate age verification data will be extremely valuable to any unscrupulous individual, or those participating in organised crime, that would seek to use that information for criminal purposes, such as identity theft, fraud or related scams. This data could also be sold by the companies capturing it to others who would seek to utilize it for targeted advertising. There is currently no acceptable or sufficient privacy code governing the use of this data by age verification providers, and no provision for this in the Bill. 12.User data, particularly where it concerns preferences for adult content, could easily be used to extort or otherwise exercise leverage over an individual. It is entirely plausible that malicious state actors could utilise such information to exert influence or control over those occupying position of political or commercial power. The value of this data is clear and would make any company possessing it particularly likely to come under cyber-attack.

chunks.text: leverage over an individual. It is entirely plausible that malicious state actors could utilise such information to exert influence or control over those occupying position of political or commercial power. The value of this data is clear and would make any company possessing it particularly likely to come under cyber-attack. 13.When viewed in this way, it is also clear that the data could be used to "out" members of the LGBT+ community who may not want their private adult preferences to be made public. The current protection of real life identity afforded by the internet has allowed people in the LGBT+ community to share experiences, form online communities and discuss sexuality whilst maintaining the protection of their real life identity. Putting that protection at risk poses a direct threat to those individuals’ safety.
chunks.display_text: 13.When viewed in this way, it is also clear that the data could be used to "out" members of the LGBT+ community who may not want their private adult preferences to be made public. The current protection of real life identity afforded by the internet has allowed people in the LGBT+ community to share experiences, form online communities and discuss sexuality whilst maintaining the protection of their real life identity. Putting that protection at risk poses a direct threat to those individuals’ safety.

chunks.text: public. The current protection of real life identity afforded by the internet has allowed people in the LGBT+ community to share experiences, form online communities and discuss sexuality whilst maintaining the protection of their real life identity. Putting that protection at risk poses a direct threat to those individuals’ safety. 14.The Bill will also create an enormous demand for age verification technology. This will disproportionately effect smaller businesses, who are less likely to be able to develop bespoke in-house solutions, and will pay more than a larger website would for third party verification on the basis that fewer verification requests will be made. The start-up trade body Coadec has warned that as many as 250,000 small and micro businesses could be impacted by the Bill. In their report entitled *How the Online Safety Bill could change liability rules, and what this means for digital firms across the UK*, Coadec state that costs for new online safety measures for smaller firms can be as much as £45 per user, whilst larger businesses will pay between £0.25 and £0.50 per user for the same service. This means that smaller firms will be faced with costs between 90 and 180 times more than their larger competitors, which will serve to stifle innovation and create significant financial barriers for start-up businesses. In the adult content sector, this would create a monopoly for MindGeek, the Canadian company that operates the website PornHub, an issue that has been reported in the Financial Times. **Alternatives**
chunks.display_text: 14.The Bill will also create an enormous demand for age verification technology. This will disproportionately effect smaller businesses, who are less likely to be able to develop bespoke in-house solutions, and will pay more than a larger website would for third party verification on the basis that fewer verification requests will be made. The start-up trade body Coadec has warned that as many as 250,000 small and micro businesses could be impacted by the Bill. In their report entitled *How the Online Safety Bill could change liability rules, and what this means for digital firms across the UK*, Coadec state that costs for new online safety measures for smaller firms can be as much as £45 per user, whilst larger businesses will pay between £0.25 and £0.50 per user for the same service. This means that smaller firms will be faced with costs between 90 and 180 times more than their larger competitors, which will serve to stifle innovation and create significant financial barriers for start-up businesses. In the adult content sector, this would create a monopoly for MindGeek, the Canadian company that operates the website PornHub, an issue that has been reported in the Financial Times. **Alternatives**

chunks.text: more than their larger competitors, which will serve to stifle innovation and create significant financial barriers for start-up businesses. In the adult content sector, this would create a monopoly for MindGeek, the Canadian company that operates the website PornHub, an issue that has been reported in the Financial Times. **Alternatives** 15.There are already certain internet service providers, mobile telephone companies and computer hardware manufacturers that block adult content websites as a default setting unless the person named on the contract, who must have banking facilities, chooses to grant access. Introducing age verification at the earliest stage (i.e. the device or internet browser) would remove the need for multiple private companies to collect and process personal data as well as creating a barrier that would prove far more difficult for young people to circumvent than website based age verification. 16.Educating parents about the availability of network level content filtering, or ‘parental controls’, to ensure those accessing the interest in a domestic setting are only able to access age appropriate content would be another sensible policy. Research by OFCOM has found that, whilst the majority of parents are aware of such features, only 27% actually use them in their homes. Similar restrictions can be applied to any device used by a child, and introducing a legal requirement that such features are enabled by default would ensure that protection would only be lost if an adult chose to disable that feature. 17.Both of these alternatives would not only be far more effective protections for children than the proposals made in the Bill, but would also not carry with them the same adverse effects outlined above. *June 2022* --- \[1\] Enclosure 1: Written submission to the Joint Committee on the Draft Online Safety Bill: https://committees.parliament.uk/writtenevidence/39312/html/

Research by OFCOM has found that, whilst the majority of parents are aware of such features, only 27% actually use them in their homes.
